It will do so by providing guidance to coders on how to assign Type 1, 2, 3 or 4 status to qualitative text including, but not limited to, peer reviewed journal articles, course syllabi, policy statements, policy analyses, media communications and policy recommendations. This classification effort complements, rather than replaces, careful qualitative application of the framework required for uncovering important nuances through deliberative analysis and reflection of highly complex text. ***What the quantitative coding effort does well is to generate useful knowledge about whether the overall patterns identified by an one study or set of studies compare to other cases outside of the study. Coding also permits assessments as to whether there are differences in the dominance of one or more problem types across time and also space – be they countries, organizations, or sectors.*** Hence, not only can a well-developed code book generate descriptions about a range of policy and governance outcomes, it will also allow for broadening epistemological and methodological techniques that might explain variation – such as the correlation between the kind of disciplinary, sub-disciplinary and interdisciplinary training experts received that shape their bias in emphasizing one Type over the other. This in turn, might help generate knowledge about, and reverse when desired, “unconscious bias” about the way policy scholars and practitioners conceive of, and measure, “on the ground” problems. In other words, this framework can help advance “fit for purpose” policy analysis. To be clear: unlike Cashore’s broader argument about the drift away from Type 4 problems that he has, and is, applying to understand their role in shaping sustainability policy and governance over the last 35 years, the code book is designed to describe a range of outcomes, to which Cashore and his collaborators, and anyone else for that matter, can better assess, test, and develop their own arguments and theories. In short, the code book, paves the way for, but does not take a position on, which of the four problem types is preferred nor the causes of changes in emphasis of the Types. Importantly, although the 4PT distinctions originally came out of Cashore’s work on global, transnational and local approaches to sustainability policy and governance, they can be applied to disentangle how any kind of problem facing society and policy making are analysed, and treated, by scholars and experts. This means that ***while Cashore applies and draws examples from sustainability policy scholarship and practice, the framework is equally useful for anyone working on problem focused policy analysis including, but not limited to those scholars working in schools of public health, education, social work, forestry, fisheries, natural resources, agriculture, and even mining and energy for that matter.*** Having said this, the guidance and examples below for coders to be able to render concrete important but sometimes abstract distinctions, draw on examples from five years of efforts to code sustainability policy and governance analyses. This code book proceeds in the following steps. First, we discuss Cashore’s “utility” and “problem solving” labels as the two key distinctions that position the 4PT framework. Second, we review Cashore’s four key schools of sustainability thought and practice – including notable strands within them – to show how each of these 4PTs are subtly nurtured by sophisticated scholarly communities. Third, we review the challenges facing coding of different kinds of qualitative documents. Fourth, we review different sorting challenges based on the kinds of document being including abstracts of course syllabi, journal articles. Fifth we offer solutions to each including reviewing of our protocol for sorting scholarly articles through careful qualitative analysis that has been developed and revised over the last five years. We then review our approach to coding course syllabi and the development of key words to facilitate swift coding of a range of documents. We then discuss our systematic efforts to advance AI assisted coding with the goal of producing systematic text analysis that would be consistent with the laborious human coding efforts of the last six years (i.e. we seek to achieve intercoder reliability between AI and humans .8 or better). We conclude by offering recommendations on next steps for both Cashore’s team and collaborators, as well as those who might want to apply the 4PT framework beyond sustainability policy and governance considerations. Appendices A reviews our approach to coding articles beyond the two distinctions as a way to generate propositions about preliminary explanations for results and Appendix B which provides examples from cases with which to assist in sorting initially “ambiguous” cases. Section 2: ## a) Key Distinctions: Utility and Problem Solving Orientations The specific coding task appears at first glance seems quite straightforward i.e. to distinguish whether scholarly and practitioner policy analyses emphasise, or not, “utility” and “problem solving” orientations (Table 1). | Table1: The Four Problem Types (4PT)
Simplified Distinctions | Utility Dominates? YES | Utility Dominates? No | | ----------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------- | --------------------- | | **Problem Solving Dominates? YES** | TYPE 1 | TYPE 4 | | **Problem Solving Dominates? NO** | TYPE 2 | TYPE 3 | These two distinctions originally emerged from Cashore’s reflections, individually and with colleagues, about why so many sustainability policy experts have disagreed for over 35 years about how to think about, and also manage, critical environmental challenges facing the planet highlighted by climate, biodiversity and forest degradation. (Again, while derived from the sustainability policy and governance cases, the 4PT distinctions are meant to be applied to any “on the ground” problems facing policy makers and society including health care, education, child care, education and so on). The origins of the 4PT distinctions followed Cashore’s recognition, having only worked in interdisciplinary forestry, environment and policy schools, that longstanding largely unconscious biases among himself, his colleagues, and experts generally, about how to study the world of sustainability policy and governance, helped explain longstanding debates about not only about how to conceive of, and measure, sustainability challenges, but also recommended courses of policy action for managing the environment, including climate, species extinctions, biodiversity loss, as well as forests and natural resources management. What Cashore noticed was two key divides within these debates. ***The first distinction*** was whether sustainability scholars and practitioners believed that enhancing **“utility”** outcomes was the key task facing sustainability policy analysis. He noted that a large group of scholars generally felt that the more utility was enhanced, the better off society will be. While this causal belief is widely diffused among a range of analysts, it is also hotly debated by scholars and analysts who either oppose utility as a guiding orientation, and/or seek other ways to frame desired outcomes. Hence, Cashore recognized that the belief that utility enhancement was the preferable, from those that did not, was an excellent way to distinguish sustainability policy and governance analyses. ***Cashore’s second distinction*** came from his recognition that while some narrowed their conclusion to assessing how to solve or effectively manage the clearly specified problem at hand- such as such as saving a particular species from extinction, avoiding resource collapse of a specified fish stock, or limiting warming to 1.5 degrees above pre-industrial levels – others brought in analytical frames that allowed for a deliberations over not only how, but ***whether*** the problem at hand could or should be solved. That is, this latter group applied adjudicating frameworks that were developed as a way to address ***any*** particular problem – regardless as to its unique features - that might find its way to the societal or policy agenda in mind. Rather than generating research and analysis to shed light on how to manage a specific policy challenge, this group was united by developing frameworks that allow for consideration of not acting at all, or acting in a way that does not effectively manage the problem at hand. Section 3: ## b) Conceptual Foundations: Utility and Problem Contingent Labels While these distinctions showed great promise, the next task was to elaborate their meanings so that their influence could be better identified and assessed. Section 4: ### The utility label covers a broad range Cashore’s 4PT framework notes that the term “utility” is generally applied and defined broadly. It includes for example, those trained in welfare economics who, largely focusing on income or economic growth, convert others outcomes to “utility equivalents” in order to monetize, and compare, human preferences for green space and nature, with their direct interest in generating income. The term “utility” also incorporates the broader philosophy of “utilitarianism” that seeks to advance outcomes that produce the “greatest good for the greatest number in the long run”. It is also used more generally as a term to discuss whether, in terms of enhancing individual or collection satisfactions, there is any “utility” in a proposed course of action? For Cashore, the rationale for use of “utility” as a label was that it best captures the general belief behind a range of analyses that their goal is to improve “**human satisfactions”.** This is an important clarification because the utility as a term has a decidedly anthropogenetic orientation in which policy is geared towards improving the interests of humans either today or in the future. This, in turn, is shaped by some kind of homage and widespread work developing theories relevant to unpacking human **“self-interest”** .What is important for coding on the 4PT framework is that the concern itself is what is important – i.e. **the utility label in the 4PT framework is shorthand to capture those who are motivated to advance human self-interested satisfactions that incorporate a range of more specific satisfaction seeking rationales** This orientation in turn, allows coders to identify efforts to promote and understand **self-interested behaviours at multiple levels** of sustainability policy and governance including individual, organizational, nation state decisions as well as those within international governance arenas and global governance agreements. Hence, the utility label includes, for coding purposes, analyses focused on aggregating **individual utility** , **organizational utility** (such as companies returning shareholder value), or even **states’ utility** by maximizing their own self-interested preferences. This category incorporates, rather than distinguishes, longstanding debates among neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars within international relations about whether self-interested, utility chasing states, are motivated to achieve relative or absolute gains. (The 4PT framework notes that debates about relative or absolute gains have undermine the similar quest to advance utility seeking interests, including, at the state level, relying on some conception of “national interest” that improves the satisfaction of its population through some kind of beneficial “rational” outcome. **Hence, this broad use of the utility label focuses coders on identifying any kind of analysis that is motivated by enhancing human satisfaction through self-interested preference maximization** . This allows coders to capture in one category a range of scholarship and analysis that although diverse and nuanced, are united by their underlying orientation in emphasizing individual, organizational and state based preferences that pay homage to improving human “satisfactions” at multiple levels. Section 5: ### The problem solving label: distinguishing analyses that target “how”, not “whether” --- The term “problem solving” is also used broadly to capture a range of expertise and approaches that undertake research with which to solve or effectively manage a particular “on the ground” problem. That is, they eschew asking “whether” a problem can be effectively managed, and instead ask, given the problem at hand, how can it be effectively managed? This category then incorporates a broad group of scholars and analysts who are united in their quest to understand and effectively management an “on the ground” problem such as the extinction of a particular species or limiting warming to no more than 1.5/2 °C above pre-industrial levels promoted through the Paris Accord. This means that the analyses they bring, and conclusions they make, will go back to implications for generating knowledge useful for problem solving of a problem, or a class of problems. Similarly, these analysts will never apply universal pre-established theories to assess whether the problem at hand ***ought*** to be solved. This is not their interest. Likewise while their research may carry lessons for other problems, they do not seek to generate universal theories that can be applied to any kind of problem. Put another way, this group of scholars would, for these reasons, avoid any quest for developing a theoretical orientation, or set of recommendations, that went beyond the problem or class of problems from which their research was derived. The practical result is that many in this category tend to carefully assess problem structure or “key features of the problem” they are attempting to shed light on how to better manage or solve. This is important. The reason they eschew any effort to develop a universal way to address any problem is because, by disconnecting from problem structure, the approach applied elsewhere might not effectively manage or solve another class of problems – i.e. it might even make those worse off. This means that they are largely conducting research in which its lessons are “problem contingent”. Any effort to generate lessons beyond a class of problems that is offered as a way to think about, and manage, all problems, is evidence that a scholarly analysis is not in this category. More specifically, any effort that concludes it is not “feasible” or “rational” to address a particular problem at hand that draws on a pre-established theory or framework that that was developed to analyse any and all problem is outside of this sorting category. This elaboration leads to the identification of a more nuanced table of which human and AI coders must be aware. Coders will note that I have also changed the Y axis label to “problem contingent” which I treat as synonymous as “problem solving” dominates, but also more useful for coding purposes. I keep the broader term in the first table as it is more accessible to the uninitiated coder, while bringing in the more specific term once they have read these first few overview pages. | Table 2: The Four Problem Types
Expanded Distinctions | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? YES | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? NO | | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? YES** | TYPE 1 Utility / Contingent | TYPE 4 Not Utility / Contingent | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? NO** | TYPE 2 Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | TYPE 3 Not Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | In sum, at the risk of repetition, let me be clear: these frameworks offer ways to deliberate over ***not only how, but whether, a specific problem can be effectively managed given a broader set of considerations beyond the problem*** itself. Section 6: ## C) Examples of Not Problem Contingent (Universal Approaches) As I discuss below I refer to the major strand of Optimization school of sustainability thought and practice known as “cost-benefit analysis” (CBA) as a leading example of this approach. This is because CBA frameworks assesses whether or not a policy intervention governing a particular problem will be able to produce, or not, aggregate utility benefits. Under this approach, if policy designers cannot find an intervention to address a problem at hand that will enhance overall utility (i.e. possible policy interventions are all deemed to undermine aggregate utility) then the “rational” answer from this CBA approach is not to act. There are, to be sure, many other strands of the Optimization school. For example, within international relations, both neo-realist and neo-liberal international relations scholars seek to generate theories of state preference, including longstanding debates about whether states maximize their preferences owing to absolute or relative gains. What is important for the 4PT framework is not their debates about underlying motivations that shape a given state’s “self-interest” preferences, but that both neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars develop their theories and frameworks to explain ***state behaviour in general*** – i.e. they seek universal understandings of utility chasing state preference that explain, but are not derived from, their choices about how to manage a specified problem. That is, they develop and apply theories of state preferences that apply beyond a particular problem, but are meant to explain state behaviour over a particular problem. To repeat, their project is to develop universal theories of state action that are not derived from, or limited to, any specific problem or issue states happen to be deliberating over. These theories of state action also inform recommendations from these scholar about how states might act go address a specific problem in order to better achieve their pre-existing self-interested preferences (i.e. how to “optimize” outcomes given existing understandings of state preferences). This is why, as I discuss below, so many IR scholars make the case for addressing the global climate crisis based on the argument that doing so will advance state preferences, rather than asking whether a theory of state preferences is the most useful way to think about, and manage, particular kinds of problems. This same dilemma facing cost-benefit scholars who so believe in the utility enhancing motivations that they constantly argue, and work so hard to demonstrate – even despite overwhelming evidence to the contrary – that an emphasis on the utility undermining effects of the climate crisis is the most useful way to conceive of, and develop policy options for, averting global warming. As I discuss below, strands within the “Compromise School” of sustainability thought and practice are also replete with a range of efforts to develop universal theories of stakeholder engagement, legitimacy formation, and conflict resolution that, while rejecting utility chasing theories, nonetheless also develop frameworks for understanding motivations of individuals, organizations and states, that are also developed to understand, and prescribe solutions for, a range of problems that may come across the desk of policy officials. For example, there is one strand of the Compromise school known as “alternative dispute resolution” ADR that offers a way for stakeholder to agree on litigation avoiding natural resources management agreements that draws on universal theories of individual and organization behaviour as a way foster a compromise agreement among disparate interests. While drawing on theories that extend far beyond, and reject, utility motivations as far to narrow an approach to human behaviour, they share the same mission in developing university theories as a way to generate an understanding about not only how, but whether, the “on the ground” issue, or set of issues, can be resolved given the overall focus on conflict resolution rather than solving and effectively managing, the “on the ground” problem that spark the ADR in the first place. Another stand of the compromise school has been nurtured by those studying philosophy which seeks to generate a set of norms and protocols for making difficult decisions – such as whether to save someone stopped on the train tracks that might risk the lives of many on the train. In these cases, pre-existing approaches for developing normative theory based protocols for doing so – not based on utility per se but on claims to higher level “ethics” are turned to as a way to have society and policy makers thinks about managing difficult questions – from species extinctions that results from enhancing economic growth – to saving lives during a pandemic when the isolationist policies for doing say may undermine children’s cognitive development. In these cases what is important for the 4PT framework is that the adjudicating analysis that result are meant to help policy makers and society make decisions to address any problem that might come along, and are not derived, narrowed to, or contingent, on a particular class of problems. Section 7: ## d) Examples of Problem Contingent Approaches (Not Universal) In contrast, and as I elaborate below, members of Commons and Prioritization schools narrowing that adjudicating frameworks to only address, and are indeed derived from, a particular “on the ground” problem or class of problems – whether it be for utility enhancing (Commons) or non-utility (Prioritization) motivations. There are many strands within each of these schools that reinforce “problem contingent” rather than non-problem contingent universal (i.e.. meant to be applied to any problems) frameworks. As I discuss below the most famous utility seeking, problem contingent strand within sustainability studies are those seeking to understand, and effectively manage, “tragedies of the commons” that, if not well managed, produce avoidable resource collapse. Here, scholars seek to understand key features of the problem – such as Ostrom’s fundamental point that some commons tragedies – such as fish that swim - cannot be served by “excluding” access – and hence require different solutions to effectively manage them. The point for the “problem contingent” classification here is that scholars like Ostrom and her students who focus on “non-excludable” tragedies, only develop theories and approaches based on these key features. That is, they do not research, and have little to say, problems that do not share these key features. There are many likeminded strands about developing problem contingent approaches by those who do not share the Common’s utility orientation, but do share is focus on how to effectively manage specified “on the ground” problems – i.e. they eschew considerations about “whether” a specified problem can be solved, to deliberating over how it can solved or effectively managed. For example, Tribe’s work on species extinctions rejected analytical universal approaches offered by extant “policy sciences” framework, to show how any consideration to saving a particular species must begin, and end, based on the knowledge about what kind of conservation efforts is required to maintain the viability of that species. While Tribe and others like him turned to debates in philosophy and the study of “ethics”, he rejected as problematic any effort to address species extinctions that might produce an outcome that caused species extinctions. Hence universal adjudicating frames about particular protocols for assessing policy dilemmas – based on universal theories of “ethics”, was in fact, highly unethical. Others in this line of thinking within international relations have pointed to the “on the ground” problem of slavery. For this group, like species extinctions, the only way to eradicate slavery to develop policies and interventions based on achieving no-slavery. The idea of applying cost-benefitanalysis, or alternative dispute resolution, to assessing not only how, but whether, slavery can be eradicated is anathema to this strands of the prioritization school because it opens the door to consider policy options in which slavery might continue. Hence for these scholars, adjudicating frameworks are always derived from knowledge to manage a specified problems (or class of problems with similar structural features and attributes, such as Ostrom’s non-excludability tragedies). These distinctions have led Cashore to elaborate four categories to include these broader distinctions (Table 2) in which we can now further narrow “problem solving” to “problem contingent” which more accurately labels theories and frameworks and course of action that are never meant to be universal, but only applicable to a class of problems Section 8: ## e) Examples of the Resulting Four Problem Types (4PT) Now that we have covered how the 4PT framework defines utility and problem solving distinctions, and reviewed examples of universal versus problem contingent approaches, we are now in a position to identify examples of approaches classified within each: Type 1 (utility, problem contingent); Type 2 (utility, not problem contingent); Type 3 (not utility, not problem contingent); Type 4 (not utility, problem contingent). This section will, out of necessity, repeat some of the examples above. This is by design: repetition of examples will provide clarity when the same ones are used to distinguish different parts of the classification framework. In addition, this part is important since the coding is focused on the resulting four distinctions – so understanding how the above review figures for making decision about where to place a range of literatures is greatly facilitated by giving these concrete examples. This review, in turn, paves the way for a broader discussion of four key schools of sustainability that reinforce, but are also much broader, than the 4PTs. While broader, inclusion of discussion of the schools is expected to generate knowledge that will facilitate and enhance and generate a nuanced approach to coding the 4PTs. Section 9: ### i. Type 1: Utility Dominates, Problem Contingent **Type 1** then sorts those that are motivated by utility chasing orientations, but narrow their focus to a specified “on the ground” problem that is seen as undermining utility enhancement. Those scholars that are sorted into this category seek to generate analyses and ideas for intervening that improve utility outcomes for that problem. These approaches target the problem or class of problems they seek to ameliorate. Their interest is not in developing universal theories to address any and all sustainability problems. This means that they generate recommendations and analyses based on their problem or class of problems in isolation from other problems that might have different features. And they certainly would never apply a theoretical or analytical framework to discuss whether the problem should be solved. There are countless examples of this kind of orientation. For example, I have a colleague who studies the issue of taxis idling looking for riders in downtown Singapore when it appears that they would make more income (utility) by driving to the airport. This problem then can be solved by reducing “information asymmetry” though some kind of intervention – such as advertising or sharing this knowledge, or through some kind of regulation reducing an ability to idle. In this case, interventions are adjudicated only for their ability to manage that particular problem - regardless as to the possible effects of the “effective policy intervention” in reducing overall aggregate utility – such as those case in which limited government resources are better placed on managing another problem that would lead to higher utility. As I discussed above and elaborate below Type 1 sorting would also include a large group of scholars and practitioners working on “collective action theory” in general, and its application to sustainability through the “tragedy of the commons metaphor” in particular. This is an important distinction. Collective Action theorists are motivate by eliminating utility undermining outcomes such as rubber necking on highways, hording of toilet paper during pandemics, or over harvesting that leads to collapse of fish stocks or water supplies. These approaches, which draw on the classic “prisoners dilemma” in which, in the absence of an ability to develop and comply with, a collective agreements, two prisoners in different cells both have a “self-interest” to confess because if they do not, but their partner in crime does, they will be handed a much higher and longer prison sentence. This “confessing/confessing” strategy is considered to the most effective utility enhancing approach given no collective agreement (an outcome known as the “Nash equilibrium) but it is not the most utility enhancing solution. The most utility enhancing solution is for both not to confess, as this will lead to lower prison sentences overall. In this case, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions to allow for both not to confess. In the sustainability world, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions such that harvesting of the resource in question is less than or equal to the biological rate or reproduction. In all these cases, analyses are focused on human satisfactions, but only around the specific problem in question. As I discussed above and in more detail below, Elinor Ostrom’s path to earning the Nobel prize in economics was sparked by her recognition that for some utility undermining tragedies, highlighted by fish, excludability was not possible. This mean that traditional interventions to solve this utility undermining commons tragedies that turned to private property or state ownership, and that assumed excludability was possible, were ill suited to addressing those utility undermining resource depletion tragedies. Ostrom then turned to assessing how to effectively manage these “common pool resources” (CPRs) which she posited were better addressed through local resource governing innovations. The point for coders here is that all of these efforts paid attention to the key features of the problem at hand – specifically those contextual and contingent factors that explained the particular utility undermining outcome for the problem at hand. Collective action theorists in general, and commons tragedy scholars in particular, only generalize based on the specific problem at hand, or a class of problems such as those commons tragedies in which excludability is not possible. They would never therefore apply their lessons to managing all problems nor bringing in an assessment as to whether the problem can be solved. They only look at the how to do so, not whether. Section 10: ### ii. Type 2: Utility Dominates, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) In contrast, **Type 2** literatures share an interest in understanding and championing utility seeking preferences, but they reject a focus that only includes attention to the specific problem at hand. The logic behind this, whether CBA that targets and aggregates individual preferences, or IR theories that target and aggregate state preferences, is that that group believes that it is unwise to assess policy options for ameliorating a specified problem without considering, and finding a way to incorporate, the result of this decision on other kinds of problems. Their logic is clear: if solving or effectively managing one specific kind of utility undermining activity – such as say, over harvesting timber – results in a set of policy and governance decisions that might preclude more lucrative activities – such as say the introduction of copper mining and its promise of increase wages – then policy officials have undermined their responsibilities in managing for the greatest amount of utility with which to benefit the welfare of citizens. This category also captures the IR scholars above who also seek to generalize about the processes through which states do, or should, maximizing their preferences – often in the name of the “national interest”- that are then applied to assess how – and sometimes even whether – the problem at hand can be effectively managed given state preferences. Hence those analysts that turn to state preferences as a way to think about how to manage and develop policy response – whether it be, as discussed below, Nordhaus’ reasoning that optimal state responses to the utility undermining impacts of the climate crisis will produce a 3.2 degree world – in contrast to the 1.5 outcome a problem contingent approach of the IPCC. It also applied, as discussed below, to neo-liberal institutionalist Keohane whose climate research turns to his theory of state preferences for policy solutions, rather than interrogating the contingent nature of the climate crisis itself. Hence whether CBA, or conflict resolution or a host of other likeminded approaches, Type 4 classifies those analytical approaches that are not offered as contingent on solving a particular problem but as a way to adjudicate the problem at hand by apply a universal way to think about, measure, and identify, and project policy interventions. Hence, these approach there expands consideration, and offering a framework for doing so, about whether the problem at hand **can, or should,** be effectively managed based on some kind of approach to preference formation. Section 11: ### iii. Type 3: Utility Does Not Dominate, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) There are many diverse approaches that are nonetheless united by their **Type 3 status** : i.e. analytical frames for understanding, explaining and prescribing policy actions that generate protocols for doing so that eschew utility motivations as a dominant force or outcome, and instead reflect on a diverse set of motivations with which to understand, and prescribe courses of action. These approaches are united by rejecting the treatment of non-utility outcomes as “irrational” and instead wonder how to understand and incorporate cultural values, social practices, environmental norms, that produce some kind of way to both understand the world and also provide the basis for recommendations about how to include a diverse set of ideas, organizations that generate legitimate process and wide accepted outcomes. As reviewed above when discussing universal approaches, while they reject utility as a dominant motivation, they share the idea that pre-existing theories and frameworks can be applied to adjudicate not only how, but whether, a particular problem can be solved. In other words, with these approaches – whether originating from a particular set of ethical frames in philosophy, sociology, geography or critical theory, one never knows for sure whether applying a particular one of these approaches to a particular problem will result in the problem at hand being solved or effectively managed. In other words, in contrast to the teleological orientation of Types 1 and 4, Type 3 approaches work from a preexisting framework – or develop a framework from a case that is meant to apply to any chase – rather than developing a framework that only pertains to that problem or class of problems. Section 12: ### iv. Type 4: Utility Does Not Dominate, Problem Contingent For these reasons **Type 4** frameworks are distinguished, like Type 1, on their ability to help understand, and ameliorate, a specified problem from the Paris Accord’s 1.5/2 degree objectives, to a particular species being threatened with extinction. However unlike Type 1 reinforcing approaches, Type 4 problems not only reject utility as the guiding force, they usually find that when those chasing utility outcomes are effective, they usually cause, or exacerbate, a range of Type 4 problems. The classic example comes to us from the identification of two distinct problems that are outside of the sustainability challenges to which Cashore devoted his empirical work. The first problem is that of slavery – i.e. the act of one human having property rights over another human being – i.e. “owning” them. A range of scholarly analyses assessing this problem have found that the only way to ensure slavery is dramatically reduced or eradicated is to begin, and end, with the required outcome: i.e. no one, for any reason, is allowed to own another person. Many apply relevant frameworks have not only found that (Type 1 and 2) utility chasing motivations was behind slavery in the first place, but that application of Type 3, 2 frames permits deliberations over whether slavery ought to be considered – which, by definition, undermines an ability to achieve the desired outcome. The second example is anti-colonial norms in which reducing or averting an attempt by one country to take over another can only be achieved by teleological approaches that beginning, and end, with the absence of colonialism. Scholarly analysis reinforcing Type 4 hence would reject neo-liberal and neo-realist IR theories as permitting an understanding of state preferences that could also consider colonial type behaviours, rather than assessing mechanisms for ensuring these possibilities are never considered. As I discuss below, a number of analytical frames within environmental studies are consistent with Type 4 reasoning. Section 13: ## f) Coding Challenges The above review will most certainly facilitate, and place in context, efforts to code qualitative documents against the 4PTs. This facilitates, rather than overcomes, several challenges that a code book must incorporate. First, while some documents may seem straightforward, others may provide a nuanced or even contradictory account that makes sorting more challenging. The solution to this is to provide, and justify, systematic protocols to facilitate sorting of what we refer to as “ambiguous” cases below. Second, different types of documents will need slightly different ways to disentangle the 4PTs. For example, our careful review of journal articles on sustainability policy and governance requires laborious coding of highly complex articles. Our original focused on the Journal of Regulation and Governance was used to both revise our 4PT distinctions, and then to go back, recode, and assess intercoder reliability. However, our approach to coding abstracts for course syllabi at Yale’s environment schools was much swifter and faster – as we only needed to see whether the course championed a utility outcome or not, and whether it was focused on applying its approach to specific on the ground problems. For example, we excluded purely science based classes from our review of Yale’s environment school, since most science based classes provided basic knowledge about problems – which might appear to be Type 4- but because they didn’t identify policy or governance solutions – where actually not part of our universe of analyses. **Article to Analyze:** Page 1: The Transformation of organic regulation: The ambiguous effects of publicization Alessandra Arcuri Erasmus School of Law, Erasmus University, Rotterdam, Netherlands Abstract The regulatory regime for organic products is different from other non-state-market driven (NSMD) regimes because it is the only one that evolved from a purely private into a regime where the establishment of minimum standards has become the monopoly of public powers. This article is the ﬁrst to study the effects of the process of publicization, a term coined to characterize the transformation of private into public standards. The central hypothesis studied is that the process of publicization has empowerment and containment effects at the same time. To test the hypothesis the article analyses the effects of publicization on regulatory capabilities of private regulators as well as on the quality of the standards. The effects of publicization are further explored by comparing the legal and institutional architecture that shapes the coexistence of private and public regimes in the EU and the US, showing important differences between the two systems. The article offers a new perspective to look at the dynamic interaction between private and public regulation and its ﬁndings are of general relevance for the debate on the desirability of governmental intervention on private regulatory schemes. Keywords: eco-labeling, non-state-market-driven (NSMD) governance system, organic standards, private regulation, voluntary standards. 1. Introduction Organic agriculture is unique among self-regulatory regimes in that it is the only one that evolved into a regime where the establishment of minimum standards has become the monopoly of public powers. Organic standards were born as an initiative of private organizations, and their production and maintenance was once the exclusive domain of these organizations. During the mid 1980s and 1990s, however, the power to deﬁne what is organic was transferred into public hands, by virtue of public regulation. Today, in order for a product to be labeled as organic, the publicly enacted standards need to be respected. I use the term publicization to capture the transformation of private into public regulation. The word publicization has been coined for the purposes of this research, as an antonym of the term privatization (Cashore 2002). While a regulatory regime involves different phases, from standard setting to enforcement, this article focuses on standard setting only. Accordingly, the term publicization is used in relation to the nature of the actor that promulgates the standards and it serves the purpose of shifting attention from the phenomenon of regulatory privatization to an inverse pattern, whereby the public regulator takes a central role in the deﬁnition of standards that started off as private ones. By private regulation, scholars often refer to the body of standards established by private actors, such as non-governmental organizations (NGOs), multinational corporations, and private standard setting bodies, sharing the feature that regulatory authority is not granted by the state (Cashore 2002; Schepel 2005; Bernstein & Cashore 2007; Cashore et al. 2007). Standards dealing exclusively with the social and environmental impacts of business operations have been referred to as non-state-market-driven (NSMD) governance systems (Cashore 2002). While several NSMD regimes – such as forest and ﬁsheries certiﬁcation programs and fair trade regimes – have been widely studied by regulation and governance scholars, organic standards have surprisingly received little attention. The transformation of the NSMD regime for organic standards into a regime dominated by public regulation shifts the attention to the interaction between public and private regulation. Several scholars have started to investigate the effects of public regulation on private regulatory regimes (Boström 2003; Cashore et al. 2004; Vogel 2008; Gulbrandsen 2014). By studying the regulatory regime for organic agriculture, this article aims to contribute to this body of scholarship. The main goal of this analysis is to qualitatively identify the effects −but not the causes −of the process of publicization, and, more particularly, examine how this process might inﬂuence the “regulatory capabilities” of different actors: that is, the choice of individuals and collectives to “frame” the regulatory regime that “shall govern them” (Cafaggi & Pistor 2014). The theoretical framework of regulatory capabilities developed by Fabrizio Cafaggi and Katharina Pistor in this special issue is aimed at understanding the “impact [Transnational Private Regulation] TPR has on the ability of individuals and collectives to determine the rules that shall govern them” and it draws attention to the risks that TPR “might systematically entrench the regulatory powers of some at the expenses of others” (Cafaggi & Pistor 2014, 16). This article expands the reach of their theoretical framework by seeking to understand how regulatory capabilities are affected once the private regulatory regime shifts into public hands. Understanding these effects is of general relevance for the normative debate on the desirability of governmental intervention in private regulatory schemes. The overarching hypothesis of this research is that the process of publicization has ambiguous empowerment and containment effects at the same time. On the one hand, the publicization of standards can be seen overall as supportive of organic agriculture. On the other hand, the “core values” of organic agriculture might have been gradually corrupted by publicization, and the regulatory capabilities of the original organic constituency progres- sively contained. Paradoxically, public regulation may be seen as having promoted private regimes, and, contextually, as having pre-empted private regulators from establishing a more radical regime. The diverse effects of the process of publicization in the ﬁeld of organic regulation present a conundrum that calls for intensive research. Given that little attention has been paid to the phenomenon of publicization, this article is mainly exploratory. It does not aim at testing hypotheses deductively; rather, it aims at identifying plausible hypotheses that necessitate additional research for further corroboration. The article relies on the study of primary and secondary sources. The value of the article is not in new empirical research, but rather in framing the phenomenon of publicization and showing the relevance of previous empirical research for its understanding.1 The article starts by introducing the regulatory regime for organic standards and placing it into the context of NSMD governance systems. It then analyses how the transition from a private to a public regime might be assessed from different perspectives. 2. Organic standards, publicization, and the dynamic interaction between public and private regulation 2.1. The emergence of organic standards as a pure non-state-market-driven (NSMD) governance system NSMD governance systems have been characterized as systems, in which the authority is not granted by the state, but by stakeholders and civil society through internal evaluative processes; the supply chain is the institution central to NSMD governance systems and compliance with the regulatory regime is always veriﬁed (Cashore 2002). The initial regulatory regimes for organic agriculture well ﬁt the deﬁnition of NSMD, as brieﬂy illustrated further. The development of organic agriculture has its roots in the late 18th and early 20th centuries, under the inﬂuence of, among others, Sir Albert Howard (Gieryn 1999), Rudolf Steiner, Lady Eve Balfour, and Jerome Irvin Rodale (Heckman 2006). While it is beyond the scope of this article to trace the intellectual foundation of organic agriculture, it is safe to say that it originates in a social movement that opposed industrial agriculture and aimed at re-embedding agriculture in ecological and human values (Guthman 1998; Raynolds 2000; Vos 2000).As the organic movement gained momentum in the early 1970s, the ﬁrst experiences of regulation of organic agriculture began to emerge. In Europe and in the United States (US), as well as internationally, several private organizations started to operate the ﬁrst certiﬁcation and standard setting schemes. In Europe, the Soil Association was one of the ﬁrst organizations to enact standards (1967), and it established a certiﬁcation system in 1973 (Soil Association 2012). Others soon followed suit (e.g. The Danish Association of Organic Farmers [Økologisk Landsforening, ØL, LOJ], the Swedish KRAV, the Italian Associazione Italiana Per L’Agricoltura Biologica [AIAB]). Likewise, private regulatory The transformation of organic regulation A. Arcuri © 2014 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 145 Page 3: bodies in the US, such as California Certiﬁed Organic Farmers (CCOF), started to produce standards and to operate the ﬁrst certiﬁcation systems in the early 1970s. Clearly these regimes did not receive their authority from the state. These private regulatory regimes tended to be informal, non-hierarchical, and mostly inclusive. Stakeholders and the civil society have been the main source of authority of these regimes. The following examples are illustrative of the phenomenon. The Danish LOJ, a well-known organic farming association, founded in 1981, was composed mainly of consumers, scientists, agronomists, and farmers (Michelsen 2001b). The same feature was equally present in the French Nature et Progrès, one of the oldest associations promoting organic values, created by different stakeholders: namely, “agronomists, doctors, farmers and consumers” (Paull 2010). Likewise, the ﬁrst Italian asso- ciations (Coordinamenti Produttori Biologici) to establish organic standards included farmers, consumers, and inspectors.2 The pattern is quite similar in the US, where the ﬁrst private certiﬁcation group, the CCOF, founded in 1973, has also been characterized as starting “as a grassroots organization of small organic farmers” (Guthman 2004a: 16). These organizations soon began to acquire a transnational dimension. The most important transnational regulatory network is the International Federation of Organic Agriculture Movements (IFOAM), founded during a meeting of ﬁve organic associations held in Versailles, France in 1972, to unite the various organic movements. The ﬁrst ﬁve members were the French Nature et Progrès, the American Rodale Press, the British Soil Association, the Soil Association of South Africa, and the Swedish Biodynamic Association (Paull 2010). IFOAM also started as an informal and non-hierarchical organization (Geier 1998) and grew rapidly. By 1975, IFOAM already had 50 members, representing 17 countries, and by 1984 the members numbered 100 from about 50 countries (Geier 1998; for an account of IFOAM’s history, see Geier 2007). Today IFOAM is arguably the most important transnational network operating in the ﬁeld of organic standard setting, with more than 750 members representing over 100 countries. IFOAM operates as the meta-regulator for the organic TPR regime. Next to its meta-regulatory activity, IFOAM remains central in the deﬁnition of what can be called “core organic values.” While it may not be easy to sharply deﬁne the core values of the organic movement, scholars have identiﬁed at least the following two: “aim for holistic view,” and “aim for sustainability” (Lund & Röcklinsberg 2001). The four basic principles adopted by IFOAM could be seen as expressing the core values of organic agriculture. IFOAM-wide membership, as well as the democratic and participatory procedures by which the principles were adopted (Luttikholt 2007), clearly stands for the shared nature of these values. The IFOAM prin- ciples are: (i) Principle of Health referring to “the health of soil, plant, animal, human, and planet as one and indivisible;” (ii) Principle of Ecology; (iii) Principle of Fairness (“Organic Agriculture should build on relation- ships that ensure fairness with regard to the common environment and life opportunities”); and (iv) Principle of Care (“Organic Agriculture should be managed in a precautionary and responsible manner to protect the health and well-being of current and future generations and the environment”) (IFOAM). These principles illustrate the holistic view that should ideally permeate organic agriculture, intended as a practice geared to simultaneously attain ecological and social goals. 2.2. NSMD regimes, publicization, and the dynamic interaction of public and private regulation NSMD regimes have been variously inﬂuenced by state policies and recent scholarship has started to investigate the dynamic interaction between public and private regulation (Bartley 2011; Eberlein et al. 2014, Gulbrandsen 2014, Overdevest & Zeitlin 2014). What is peculiar to the interaction between public and private regulation is that many NSMD governance regimes emerged as a reaction to the lack of state action to manage environmental problems and unfairness along the supply chain. For instance, the Forest Stewardship Council (FSC) was estab- lished in the aftermath of the failed negotiations on an international treaty for sustainable forest management at the Earth Summit in 1993 (Meidinger 2006; Overdevest & Zeitlin 2014). Likewise, the Marine Stewardship Council (MSC) was established “in response to increasing concerns over the inability or unwillingness of gov- ernments to resolve the challenges of ﬁsheries management” (Gulbrandsen 2014, 80). Fair trade schemes and the ﬁrst organic private regulatory regimes are no exceptions. The lack of public regulation in these realms can be seen as the denial of a regulatory space to constituencies positively valuing sustainability, fair trade, and environ- mental protection. Thus, when assessing the effects of state policies on NSMD regimes, it is worth emphasizing that their emergence is arguably a result of the reaction of a constituency under-represented in the public The transformation of organic regulation A. Arcuri © 2014 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 146 Page 4: regulatory space. The NSMD systems, by establishing standards and principles in these ﬁelds, become competitors of the regulatory state. This competitive relation may be seen as a source of the dynamic interaction between public and private regulation. In several instances, private regimes have triggered the establishment of public policies that either endorse or support the NSMD regime. The regulatory governance of forestry offers various examples of how states have adopted public policies that directly or indirectly support private regulatory regimes. For instance, several jurisdictions have implemented sustainable timber public procurement policies that, directly or indirectly, sup- ported the FSC certiﬁcation regime. Gulbrandsen (2014) has shown how state policies often resulted in the uptake of private certiﬁcation programs for both the sustainable management of ﬁsheries and forestry. Some public procurement policies, such as the one adopted by the Dutch government, have established mechanisms of public comparison and benchmarking which have not only favored the diffusion of certain labeling regimes (e.g. FSC), but have also led to the improvement of some standards, as documented by Gulbrandsen 2014 in the case of the Programme for the Endorsement of Forest Certiﬁcation (PEFC). Similarly, the adoption of Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) guidelines on ﬁsheries eco-labeling in 2005 is likely to have enhanced the credibility of the MSC certiﬁcation system (Gulbrandsen 2014).Also in the ﬁeld of fair trade, public procurement policies have been widely used to support fair trade regimes (for an overview of the European Union [EU] and EU Member States policies, see Marin-Duran & Cremona 2012). The phenomenon of publicization can be understood as a particular manifestation of the interaction between private and public regulation. Publicization is a benchmark case because the state claims the monopoly on the establishment of minimum standards. In light of this literature it becomes interesting to ask whether the phenom- enon of publicization is supportive of the private regime. Addressing this question might be of relevance for other areas. In fact, several proposals to publicize the fair trade regimes, in a similar fashion to organic standards, have been advanced both in the EU and in the US. In its 2009 Communication, the European Commission has reiterated “the importance of maintaining the non-governmental nature of Fair Trade and other similar sustainability schemes throughout the EU,” (p. 10) arguing that “[p]ublic regulation could interfere with the workings of dynamic private schemes” (Commission of the European Communities 2009). Notwithstanding this position and the fact that national publicization proposals remain largely unadopted, the debate on the desirability of such publicization process is still open (Marin-Duran & Cremona 2012). By looking at the potential effects of publicization, this article provides valuable insights for this debate. 2.3. Understanding the effects of publicization on regulatory capabilities: a multidimensional analysis While there are countless academic studies on organic markets and standards, the dynamic interaction of public and private regulation in this ﬁeld remains relatively understudied. In order to ﬁll the gap, this article focuses on the phenomenon of publicization and identiﬁes three dimensions relevant for understanding the effects of publicization on regulatory capabilities. As brieﬂy illustrated in the previous section, public policies have often supported NSMD governance systems. In light of these results, it is worth studying in what ways publicization might be supportive of private regulatory schemes. The ﬁrst dimension considered in this study is, thus, the supportive (or enabling) potential of publicization. This is an important starting point because publicization, which by deﬁnition establishes the state monopoly on minimum standard setting, might be seen as a means to curb the regulatory capabilities of the private regulators who originally created the NSMD governance system. The ﬁrst part of this article, drawing on economic theory, will show that publicization, in spite of monopolizing minimum standards, carries with it an enabling potential for the private regulator. Secondly, state policy may affect the quality of the standards.As seen in other NSMD regimes, under the inﬂuence of public policy, standards can be watered down or become more stringent. For instance, in the ﬁeld of forest management, Gulbrandsen (2014) has illustrated how public procurement policies (most particularly in the Neth- erlands and in the UK) have stimulated PEFC to adopt more stringent standards. Assessing the effects of the publicization process on the quality of the standards is particularly relevant in the ﬁeld of organic agriculture because of the holistic values underpinning the original regime. Looking at whether publicization has weakened or strengthened organic standards may be a good proxy to assess whether publicization limits the regulatory capabili- ties of the “organic constituency” (the analytical category of organic constituency is deﬁned below). This is because, The transformation of organic regulation A. Arcuri © 2014 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 147 Page 5: by deﬁnition, the organic constituency aspires to maintain standards in line with the core organic values. Standards diverging from these values might suggest that the regulatory capabilities of the organic constituency have been constrained. Finally, the last part of this article evaluates the contestability of the regulatory regime for organic standards. By this term I mean the space left open to a critical constituency for challenging the regime. Private regulators may challenge the public regulatory regimes in different ways. They can do so by participating directly or indirectly in the regulatory process. But they can also compete with the public regulators by setting more stringent standards. It is plausible to assume that if a private labeling scheme gains more legitimacy than the public one, it becomes a serious threat to the public regime. As contestability is important for regulatory capabilities (Cafaggi & Pistor 2014), this article identiﬁes the rules likely to have an impact on the contestability of the regime. In order to analyze how the process of publicization has inﬂuenced regulatory capabilities, it is also important to identify the relevant actors. A ﬁrst distinction can be made between public and private regulators. At ﬁrst glance, publicization can be seen as restricting the regulatory capabilities of private actors, by pre-empting the latter from deﬁning minimum standards. However, as will be discussed below, the relationship between publicization and regulatory capabilities is less linear than it ﬁrst appears. Another distinction is between the constituencies supporting organic and conventional agriculture. By constitu- ency, I refer to a collectivity including farmers, businesses, NGOs, consumers, and citizens, who have an interest in the development of organic or conventional agriculture. Analyzing the effects of publicization in terms of regulatory capabilities for these two groups (i.e. the organic and the conventional constituency) may be problematic. The major problem derives from the fact that the standards themselves deﬁne what is organic; thus, a change in the standards may allow a conventional farmer to become organic. To avoid this problem, this article uses the category of “core organic values” as a benchmark, as deﬁned at the end of section 2.1. Thus, by “organic constituency” I refer to the collectivity that is committed to the realization of these core values. 3. The enabling potential of publicization 3.1. The publicization of private organic standards While private organic standards were mushrooming in the late 1970s, in the 1980s the deﬁnition of minimum organic standards had become the exclusive business of public regulators.3 Both in Europe and in the US, regulations were ﬁrst adopted at the national or local level.4 The main features of the publicization of organic standards are: a. centralization of the standard setting process; b. bureaucratization; c. codiﬁcation of standards focused on input substitution; d. institutionalization of third-party certiﬁcation to the exclusion of alternative forms of certiﬁcation. In 1991, the European Community adopted its ﬁrst regulation in the ﬁeld of organic agriculture: Council Regulation 2092/91. The ofﬁcial reasons for adopting this regulation included the promotion of markets for organic products and the integration of the European market for these products (stated in the Regulation’s Preamble). The different regulatory regimes existing in Europe before Regulation 2092/91 meant that products could not be easily traded in Europe, as they would have to comply with multiple standards. The Regulation established a set of minimum standards for the production of organic products. Most importantly, it established a positive list of products that could be used as fertilizers and plant-protection substances; apart from exceptional cases, no other products could be used in the organic method of production (Article 6 Council Regulation 2092/91). The scope of Regulation 2092/91 has been broadened by more than 25 amendments. In 2004, after a process of extensive consultations, the Commission launched the European Action Plan for Organic Food and Farming, which culminated in the adoption of Council Regulation 834/07. The major changes in the Regulation include5: a. the adoption of a new European logo, which is now mandatory for all organic prepackaged food products produced in Europe; b. the explicit prohibition of genetically modiﬁed organisms (GMOs) in organic products; c. the consolidation of the broader scope of the Regulation, which includes products from agriculture, livestock, aquaculture, seaweed, and organic yeast. Arcuri © 2014 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 148 Page 6: The US regulation is in many respects similar to the European one (for a comparative analysis, see Winickoff & Klein 2011). In the mid-1980s, the idea of establishing federal rules gained consensus, also among representatives of the movement. However, reasons for the adoption of federal regulations for organic agriculture remain largely unexplored in the literature. One author suggests that a couple of motives may have driven the process that has led to the adoption of federal rules (Vos 2000). First, federal regulations should have minimized consumer confusion amid a burgeoning growth of organic labels. Likewise, the regulations should have avoided “cheating:” that is, farmers improperly labeling their products as organic. Most curiously, another possible reason for the adoption of federal rules may have had to do with fears that the US Department of Agriculture (USDA) might ban the term “organic” because of its growing popularity being perceived as a threat to conventional agriculture (Vos 2000, p. 247). The Organic Foods Production Act (OFPA), adopted in 1990, initiated the process that led to the adoption of the US National Organic Program (NOP) in 2002. The OFPA mandated the establishment of the National Organic Standards Board (NOSB), an advisory body in charge of recommending the rules to be promulgated by the USDA. NOSB membership included organic farmers and handlers, representatives of consumer interest groups, and a few experts, and it held wide consultations to reach its set of recommendations, which“were closely aligned with IFOAM and EU standards” (Winickoff & Klein 2011, 236). Apart from some differences, both systems share the fundamental characteristic of developing the standards around the idea of input substitutions: namely, the fact that organic production processes should limit the use of non-organic inputs.6 In addition, both systems regulate the monitoring phase by allowing only third-party certiﬁ- cation. Notably, under both systems public standards do not change the voluntary nature of the standards. Only if they choose to produce organic products do they have to comply with the standards set by public law. The publicization does not mean that private organizations have been completely driven out of the regulatory space. As mentioned earlier, this article focuses only on the process of standard setting, leaving outside its scope the enforcement phase. It is worth emphasizing that beyond the phase of standard setting, the organic regime is as hybrid as many other NSMD regimes. Enforcement of the standards is normally delegated to private institutions (e.g. private certiﬁcation bodies) that are regulated by the combination of private rules (e.g. ISO 65 and EN 45011) and public authority (e.g. certiﬁcation is commonly subjected to ministerial authorization). Moreover, public standards may further co-exist with private standards (e.g. IFOAM Standards, the Demeter standards for Biody- namic products, and so on; see section 5.2 for an overview). While the contemporary world of organic standards can be seen overall as a complex mix of networked and multi-stakeholder governance along with public and private regulation, public input in the deﬁnition of the standards remains a central feature of the regime. 3.2. Publicization: pre-empting to enable? At ﬁrst sight, the fact that the establishment of minimum organic standards has become the monopoly of public powers seems to suggest that the regulatory capabilities of private actors are irremediably curtailed. While this reasoning seems straightforward, it is ﬂawed in that it overshadows the enabling potential of public regulation. To better appreciate why, it is important to understand that one important function of the organic regulatory regime is to deﬁne what is organic and, symmetrically, when the term organic cannot be used. The publicization of organic standards may promote the regulatory capabilities of private regulators and of the organic constituency in the sense that, without public regulation, the regulatory initiatives of these actors may not be sustainable. Economics provides a theoretical explanation as to why public regulation may positively affect the private regulatory regime for organic products. In economic terms, these products are credence goods: that is, goods for which it is not possible to assess their quality even after purchase (Darby & Karny 1973). Economics of information teaches us that where information about the quality of the traded goods is not readily available, adverse selection may result in market failure; even worse, high-quality goods may disappear from the market unless corrective mechanisms are implemented (Akerlof 1970). In the case of organic products, this may mean that −in the extreme −organic producers will be driven out of the market if no protection of the term is granted. From this perspective, the enactment of public standards protecting the market for organic products confers beneﬁts on organic producers and potential organic consumers. Even if market failure caused by the credence quality of organic products could possibly be solved through private regulation (Grolleau & BenAbid 2001), the case of organic agriculture is particularly complex. This is because The transformation of organic regulation A. Arcuri © 2014 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 149 Page 7: the meaning of organic is highly contested. As public regulation deﬁning the term is lacking, anybody could use the term and broaden its scope, without fearing sanctions under fraud laws, because anybody can claim a different meaning. Public protection of the term organic would solve the problem of its credence quality. Private actors could possibly remain in the regulatory space for the deﬁnition of organic standards by creating “organic plus” standards. From this perspective, publicization could have a positive impact on the capabilities of private regulators and the organic constituency, because it enables these actors to maintain the regulatory regime that they originally estab- lished and allows them to expand it in new directions. In this context, it is interesting to observe that public legal status of organic products lent additional authority to private regulatory regimes for organic agriculture. Before the term had been protected by public law, certain public and private institutions displayed a degree of formal hostility towards organic agriculture. In Italy, for instance, a Ministerial Circular in 1986 prohibited the marketing of organic products, absent a national legislation. At the micro-level, public authorities had seized some organically grown products because, in their view, in the absence of public regulation, organic products could not be circulated. Likewise, some supermarkets refused to commercialize products of organic aquaculture when there were no public rules in this area (e.g. a Coop in Emilia Romagna).7 The publicization of rules and standards that govern “organic” agricultural production changed this scenario, enabling private actors to enact organic standards. Something similar occurred in the US, where the USDA “forbade any claims that livestock was organically raised,”when a federal law on this issue was still lacking (Guthman 2004a, p. 114).8 Thus, organic livestock was not only unprotected, but also opposed by public institutions in favor of conventional livestock farming. This means that the private regulators were de facto prevented from creating effective standards for organic livestock. While it is not possible to generalize from the American and Italian cases presented here, it is safe to conclude that at least in some instances, public regulation may have enhanced the regulatory capabilities of the organic constituency. 4. Publicization and the quality of the standards At a different level of inquiry, however, the story appears less linear. As stated above, organic agriculture typically started as grassroots movements that opposed some industrial features found in conventional agriculture (Kaltoft 2001; Guthman 2004a). In this sense, organic agriculture emerged not only as a mode of production, but aspired towards social and normative transformations. Focusing on this dimension, scholars have conceptualized the organic regulatory regime as a means to re-embed markets in environmental and social values (Raynolds 2000). Several scholars have argued that organic agriculture is losing its distinctive critical features and is becoming more similar to conventional agriculture (Buck et al. 1997; Guthman 2004a and 2004b; De Witt & Verhoog 2007; Dupuis & Gillon 2009; Howard 2009; Jaffee & Howard 2010). Rural sociologists have called this phenomenon “convention- alization,”deﬁning it as“a process”by which“agribusiness is ﬁnding ways to industrialize organic production”(Buck et al. 1997, 4). Conventionalization, thus, refers to an intensiﬁed and capitalist mode of production, distant from the core values of organic agriculture. The conventionalization thesis has been amply discussed and there is disagree- ment among scholars as to the degree by which it manifests in different countries, as well as to its inevitability (Hall & Mogyorody 2001; Michelsen 2001a; Lockie & Halpin 2005; Best 2008; Tomlinson 2008). The scholarship on conventionalization is relevant for the purposes of this study because it suggests a weakening of the quality of organic standards. Given our deﬁnition of organic constituency (see section 2), the weakening of the standards could be interpreted as the result of a process that reduces the capabilities of this constituency to frame the regulatory regime for organic agriculture. Thus, it becomes relevant to understand whether, under publicization, standards have been weakened, as the conventionalization thesis seems to suggest. Let me state from the outset that the hypothesis suggested here is that a process of standards’ weakening has co-evolved with publicization. However, it remains unclear the extent to which publicization may be the cause. Jaffee & Howard 2010 have argued that in the US standards have been gradually watered down. This is well illustrated by the broadening of the positive list of allowed substances in organic agriculture. In the US, a number of controversial inputs, such as sodium nitrate and several synthetics, have been allowed. To give a sense of the phenomenon, in the last 10 years, the number of nonorganic substances allowed in the American National List has almost tripled (from 77 to more than 250; Strom 2012). Allowing many nonorganic substances appears in contrast The transformation of organic regulation A.
