More generally, this is a process that supports the industrialization of organic production, because these substances enable corporate players to more cheaply enter the market of organic agriculture. An illustrative example is the use of nitrites for salami and the like, which are preservatives known to be necessary for large industrial players that aim at selling good-looking products in supermarkets.9 Another rule, evidencing the dilution of standards’ integrity is the one – adopted in both the US and the EU regime – allowing separate farm units not to be entirely managed following organic methods. Such a rule clearly confers advantages on an intensiﬁed mode of production. More generally, the under-inclusive nature of the public regulatory regime contrasts with the aim for the holistic view and sustainability characterizing the core organic values. As seen above, both the US and EU regimes focus on inputs. By the same token, both regimes exclude social and normative issues, ranging from farm scale to fair trade. As put by Guthman: “by focusing the debates on allowable inputs, regulation has preempted concerns with broader agronomic and provisioning processes” (Guthman 1998, p. 145). The choice to exclude these issues from the regulatory regime arguably betrays important normative values, including the aim for an holistic approach to production and the Principle of Fairness endorsed by IFOAM. While the rules in themselves show that, under public regulation, standards have been watered down, one important question to be asked is whether the weakening of the standards would also have occurred had the regime remained in the exclusive hands of private regulators. This question is difﬁcult to answer because of the lack of counterfactuals. Yet some data show that if weakening of the standards occurs, it might have happened irrespective of the process of publicization. The seminal work by Guthman shows that compromises were already made to accommodate the interests of industrial producers by private regulators (Guthman 2004a). Guthman’s account of the permissibility of sodium nitrate by the private CCOF is illustrative of this phenomenon: “Perhaps the most controversial material was Chilean or, sodium nitrate . . . At one point, three of CCOF’s largest growers . . . threatened a walkout if CCOF were to ban the use of sodium nitrate. Because of this controversy, CCOF lagged behind most other certiﬁers in disallowing it” (Guthman 2004a, p. 124). According to the author, the federal rule followed the CCOF standard. This case indicates that, while it is undisputable that, under publicization, standards have been watered down, a similar phenomenon may also have occurred when standards would have remained private. Moreover, scholars have shown that the fair trade regimes have undergone a similar process in which standards have been watered down (Jaffee & Howard 2010). Interestingly enough, fair trade has never been subjected to public regulation. Jaffee and Howard (2010), in comparing organic and fair trade standards, have explained the similar trend of watering down the standards with the “parable of the risk of rapid growth”: “. . . the scaling up has created a positive feedback loop, driving the process of institutionalization in governance bodies, strengthening the hand of new larger corporate players who are best able to meet the new demand, and paving the way for capture” (Jaffee & Howard 2010, p. 395). The arguments presented above indicate that it may be difﬁcult to establish a causal link between the process of publicization and the weakening of the standards. It is still plausible to maintain that the publicization of standards may facilitate the transformation of organic agriculture into an agricultural practice dominated by corporate players, with little attention to its original “core values.” One hypothesis explaining why governments might facilitate the adoption of standards misaligned with core organic values is that organic agriculture implies a critique of the socio-political establishment. As put by Allen and Kovach, “. . . the defetishization of commodities found in the organic industry often reaches beyond the relations of production of organic food and into the relations of production of regulatory science – those scientiﬁc and governmental institutions that assure consumers that conventionally grown food is safe for people and the environment” (Allen & Kovach 2000, p 227, emphasis added). Against this background, it can be assumed that public powers try to tame the critical potential inherent to the organic world, by weakening the standards. This hypothesis, however, needs further empirical investigation to be corroborated. 5. Publicization and the contestability of the organic regulatory space 5.1. Resistance While standards have been watered down, the regulatory history of organic products seems to suggest that through the process of publicization, the organic constituency has also been able to resist the subversion of some of its core The transformation of organic regulation A. Arcuri © 2014 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 151 Page 9: values. This can be inferred from the process in both the US and in the EU that has led to the prohibition of GMOs. The original set of (proposed) regulations did not prohibit GMOs. Yet GMOs are widely considered antithetical to organic agriculture and in contrast with core values of organic agriculture (IFOAM 2002). Both in Europe and in the US, GMOs have eventually been banned; a GMO-free public organic standard was the result of contestation. In the US, the process that led to the prohibition of GMOs in organic agriculture was the result of a popular protest against the USDA. On the basis of the 1990 Organic Food Production Act, the USDA, notoriously unsym- pathetic toward organic products (Vos 2000), was in charge of establishing organic standards at the federal level. The ﬁrst set of standards crafted by the Agency allowed the infamous Big Three (GMOs, irradiation, and sewage sludge) into organic agricultural practices, in sharp contrast to what was proposed by the NOSB. The proposal was deemed so outrageous that almost 300,000 comments were submitted to the USDA; the proposal was eventually withdrawn, and today US standards prohibit the Big Three. The (administrative) right to submit public comments has proved crucial to enable contestation of public regulation by the more committed organic constituencies. GMOs were not explicitly prohibited in the ﬁrst European regulation (Regulation 2092/91); the question of whether GMOs were allowed in the organic value chain emerged in 1993, when the Commission adopted a Regulation that admitted their use in organic products under certain circumstances. The Regulation had been challenged by the Parliament before the Court of Justice, and the Court found that it was legal for the Commission to include GMOs in its implementing regulation.10 In the aftermath of this case in 1999, a new Council Regulation established that GMOs (and GMO products) were incompatible with the organic production method.11 This case shows that when the standards became public, an attempt was made in both the US and the EU to subvert the core values of organic agriculture by allowing GMOs in the value chain; however, in both cases the subversion was averted. From this, it is plausible to conclude that, if publicization can enable a process by which standards are watered down, it may be more difﬁcult to radically undermine the core values of the organic movements. This part of the regulatory history suggests that, under publicization, the organic constituency was able to let its voice be heard, and, accordingly, the regulatory capabilities of the organic constituency may not have been dramatically impaired. The procedural rights to comment on the regulatory process, as well as the rights to judicially challenge regulation, might, thus, be seen as institutional mechanisms enabling contestation. 5.2. Coexistence Within the realm of the organic regulatory space, standards set by private bodies are a potential challenge −at least discursively −to the boundaries of public regulation. This is not to say that private regulation is the only means by which the organic constituency can contest and inﬂuence the regulatory process. Procedural rights and rights to challenge regulation before courts are different means to enable regulatory contestation (as evidenced by the analysis above). This last section, however, aims at illustrating the role of private regulators in this system and how different institutional architectures can empower or dis-empower these actors. A comparison between the European and the US regime clearly illustrates how the regulatory capabilities of private standard setters can be severely restricted. In Europe, the system is multilayered, with European standards forming the common denominator; however,private standard setters are free to set standards stricter/higher than the European ones.The US legislation,by contrast,has imposed a sort of ceiling on the regulatory standards set by private regulators. In practice, private organizations can be accredited to certify “USDA organic” only if they themselves do not set standards higher than those of the National Organic Program (NOP) (cfr. NOP regulation, Subpart F, para 205.501[b][2]). This means that a farmer wanting to comply with higher standards would have to face double costs of certiﬁcation: ﬁrst, a USDA accredited organization (say CCOF) would have to be paid for the USDA organic certiﬁcation; second, another private organic association would have to be paid for the certiﬁcation of higher standards. Within this system, the typical associations that started to produce organic standards in the US, such as CCOF or the Northeast Organic Farming Association of Vermont, are driven out of the area of standard setting. Legally speaking, public regulation does not prevent private regulators from setting alternative standards; however, it substantially increases the costs and, therefore, the regulatory burdens for private regulation to compete with the public regime. Such a rule can be seen as a way to constrain the regulatory capabilities of the arguably more committed organic constituency, because it increases the costs of private organizations willing to set standards higher than the statutory ones. In other words, by eliminating a regulatory space of contestation, it reduces the degree of contestability of the regulatory regime. Private regulators wanting to push for a different regulatory agenda have to The transformation of organic regulation A. Arcuri © 2014 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 152 Page 10: exit the regime and establish an alternative one, outside the realm of organic.12 As a matter of fact, in the US there is no private regulator setting standards higher than the USDA organic regime within the realm of organic regulation. The case of organic standards suggests that public regulation may emerge in response to private standards, and in some cases, publicization might suppress private standards tout court. In other words, public regulation might be shaped in the“shadow of private regulation,”here deﬁned as a phenomenon diametrically opposite to what has been called “shadow of hierarchy.” By “shadow of hierarchy,” scholars refer to the hypothesis according to which private standards emerge in response to the threat of public regulation (Héritier & Eckert 2008). The US institutional architecture bears witness to the fact that the “shadow of private regulation” might lead to the public preemption of stringent private regulation. The European institutional architecture, however, shows that coexistence is possible and that private standards might continue to proliferate next to public law, possibly enhancing the credibility of the regime. By contrast with the US, in Europe, several associations continue to be active in producing standards. Before engaging in a compara- tive analysis of the main private standard setting activities in Europe, let me present a concrete case to illustrate how private standards have been used to inﬂuence public regulation in Europe. The history that led to the adoption of the European rules on organic wine is a clear case where private standard setters have inﬂuenced the public regulatory process. In Europe, wine can be labeled as organic only as of 2012, by virtue of EU Implementing Regulation 203/2012. Before 2012, wines could be labeled as grown with organic grapes, but could neither be called organic nor marketed with the EU organic logo. In 2004, the European Commission launched the Organic Action Plan with the goal of establishing new rules for organic products, including standards on organic wine. In 2009, the European Standing Committee for Organic Farming (SCOF) elaborated a legislative proposal on the basis of recommendations outlined by the EU funded research project,“OrWine.”In turn, this study was conducted by members of organic associations (some acting as standard setters domestically), meaning that the proposal was heavily inﬂuenced by the organic movement.13 The public regulatory process, however, was deadlocked because of a lack of agreement (mainly on the reduction in the use of sulphites) within the SCOF, and on 16 June 2010 the Commission withdrew its proposal. Several European organic associations, particularly disappointed by this result, decided to establish private standards in this area and created the European Organic Wine Carta (EOWC) (Triantafyllidis et al. 2012). The associations involved in establishing the Charter have explicitly acknowledged that they used the Charter as a mechanism for “pushing the EU politicians to restart the discussion about this topic” (emphasis added).14 This Charter, which was basically reproducing the Commission proposed regulation, has arguably functioned as a trigger for the public regulatory process to resume, so that the EU regulation could be ﬁnally adopted in 2012 (Commission Implementing Regulation (EU) 203/12). As the case of the organic wine standards shows, private standard setting can be used strategically in the regulatory space. Private regulators can challenge the legitimacy of public regulation by enacting more stringent private standards.
